transcript:
Here's a field of words — love, feel, curious, uncertain, drawn to — scattered across a dark background, each one blurred and trembling like it's being viewed through heat haze.

Look at how the piece sorts its own vocabulary into a hierarchy of wear. In the inherited words object near the top, words get grouped as threadbare, stretched, broken-in, or stiff — and that classification isn't just labeling, it drives everything downstream. Threadbare words like "love" and "heart" get five layered ghost copies behind them and a visible erosion pass in the draw word layer function, actual faded duplicates simulating wear-through. But words like "drawn to" and "processing" — the ones closer to how this AI might describe its own operation — stay stiff, getting a thin crisp outline instead, literally holding an unworn edge because, as the code puts it, "I haven't broken them in yet."

So the trembling isn't random noise — it's proportional to how borrowed each word feels. The most human words shake the most.

cues.context: hierarchy of wear. In the inherited words object near the top, words
visible erosion pass in the draw word layer function, actual faded duplicates simulating

cues.target: Stiff words show their unworn edges — I haven't broken them in yet
cues.context: as the code puts it, "I haven't broken them in yet." So the trembling isn't random
